name: Cidambarananda
dates_notes: Dated by his position in the Kashmirian / South-Indian Krama transmission chain between Hrasvanātha (tenth–eleventh century) and Maheśvarānanda (thirteenth century, who treats the Cidambarānanda lineage as the immediate predecessor of his own Mahārtha synthesis). The Mahānaya-Prakāśa survives in two distinct recensions whose mutual relation is unsettled; the corpus follows Rastogi's placement of the Cidambarānanda recension in the eleventh–twelfth century.
core_thesis: Cidambarānanda is the principal commentator on the *Mahānaya-Prakāśa* — the systematic Mahānaya (Great Way) articulation of the Krama-Kālīkula tradition. The Mahānaya is the Krama's mature self-presentation as a complete Śākta non-dualism distinct from the Trika and from the Pratyabhijñā, with its own scriptural canon (the *Krama-Sadbhāva*, the *Devī-Pañcaśataka*, and the related Krama-tantras), its own twelve-Kālī ontology, and its own initiatory transmission. The *Mahānaya-Prakāśa* — a Sanskrit verse-treatise in nine *prakaraṇas* — articulates the doctrine systematically: the metaphysics of the twelve Kālīs as the structure of consciousness, the four-fold cycle (*udaya*, *sthiti*, *saṃhāra*, *anākhya*) and the four-fold positional schema (*bāhya*, *antar*, *sthāna*, *prāṇa*), the *cakra-pūjā* of the twelve Kālīs, the *mantra-cosmology* of the Mahānaya, and the recognitional soteriology in which liberation consists in the *vimarśa* (discriminative awareness) of each Kālī as one's own nature. Cidambarānanda's commentarial work — including verse-commentary and prose exposition preserved in the lineage manuscript tradition — is the principal interpretive vehicle through which the *Mahānaya-Prakāśa*'s doctrine is articulated for school-internal transmission. His significance for the Sarvāmnāya / six-āmnāya integration tradition is that he is the principal Krama voice in the lineage chain whose terminus is Maheśvarānanda's *Mahārtha-Mañjarī*; the Mahānaya/Mahārtha synthesis is the south-Indian flowering of the Kashmirian Krama, and Cidambarānanda is the indispensable intermediate figure.

engaged_works.title: Mahanaya-Prakasa (with Cidambarananda's commentary)
engaged_works.ascription_notes: The *Mahānaya-Prakāśa* survives in two distinct recensions (the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition and a second recension preserved separately); the commentarial layer attributed to Cidambarānanda is preserved in the lineage manuscript tradition. The precise authorship of the root verses and the relation between root and commentary are debated; the corpus follows Sanderson and Rastogi in treating Cidambarānanda as the principal lineage-internal commentator and partial systematizer.

ascription_evidence.description: Rastogi's *Krama Tantricism of Kashmir* (1979); Sanderson's essays on the Krama; the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition of the Mahānaya-Prakāśa.

engaged_works.summary: A Sanskrit verse-treatise in nine *prakaraṇas* with Cidambarānanda's commentarial layer, articulating the Mahānaya-Krama doctrine systematically. The opening *prakaraṇas* establish the metaphysics of the twelve Kālīs and the four-fold cycle (*udaya* — rising; *sthiti* — abiding; *saṃhāra* — resorption; *anākhya* — the unspeakable beyond). The middle *prakaraṇas* develop the four-fold positional schema (*bāhya*, *antar*, *sthāna*, *prāṇa*) and the *cakra-pūjā* of the twelve Kālīs. The closing *prakaraṇas* develop the recognitional soteriology — liberation as the *vimarśa* of the Kālī-sequence as one's own nature — and the *mantra-cosmology* of the Mahānaya. The work is the principal Sanskrit textual vehicle of the Mahānaya-Krama and the immediate substrate of Maheśvarānanda's *Mahārtha-Mañjarī-Parimala*.

reception_notes: Cidambarānanda's commentarial work is the principal interpretive vehicle through which the *Mahānaya-Prakāśa* enters the Mahārtha synthesis articulated by Maheśvarānanda in the thirteenth-century South India. The lineage chain Hrasvanātha → Cidambarānanda → Maheśvarānanda is the spine of the Krama / Mahānaya / Mahārtha tradition. Modern scholarship (Sanderson; Rastogi; Padoux) treats Cidambarānanda as a partly obscure but indispensable transmissional figure; the lineage-internal evidence is dominant since the Krama transmission was restricted-initiatory rather than open scholastic.
